author: tansy

body:
Fair enough. There are other ways in which the command-line syntax doesn't fully behave as GNU-ishly as one would expect, basically because of the Python command line parsing module used.

Maybe someone will find a way to fix this and then we could have a `--gnuishly-correct` option together with which `--version` could include the program name and possibly other information such as licensing and versions of dependencies (eg _ffmpeg_).

The same issue still applies in _yt-dlp_:
```console
$ ~/Downloads/yt-dlp-2024.07.02 --version
2024.07.02
$ 
```
author: dirkf

body:
I try to reason it this way - it makes sense to announce program name when asking for a version. Cause version of what? It's especially true here, where date is used as a version. What do you think when you see this thing: \`2024-01-01', cause I see a date. What that date refers to is not known.
What do you think when you see this: \`gzip 1.13'? First think that comes to a mind is gzip, version 1.13.
On the other hand, I referred to [GNU coding standards](https://www.gnu.org/prep/standards/html_node/_002d_002dversion.html) as they are simple and concise. Other programs include all kinds od unnecessary rap to their version print. So this was the simplest and most logical of all.

> The same issue still applies in yt-dlp

Anecdotal evidence. Hundreds of other programs do include their name in version print.

> Maybe someone will find a way to fix this and then we could have a --gnuishly-correct option together with which --version could include the program name and possibly other information such as licensing and versions of dependencies (eg ffmpeg).

I quoted GNU as the option is \`--version' brings to mind GNU coding standards rather than any other 'code of conduct'.

If it was put in a function that would do it, would it be better?
I can do it so it would print \`program-name version' in basic option and, in verbose, it would add some more stuff. Maybe like this:

```
$ youtube-dl --version
youtube-dl 2011-11-11

$ youtube-dl --version --verbose
youtube-dl 2011-11-11
Copyright/left (C) 2011
This is free and unencumbered software released into the public domain.
For more information, please refer to <http://unlicense.org/>
There is NO WARRANTY, to the extent permitted by law.
```

and in help:

```
    -V, --version                        Print program version and exit. Verbose output prints also licence.
```

Where shall I put this function, in \`version.py' or \`utils.py' or somewhere else?
And what is \`action='store_true''? \`dest='update_self'' is, as I guess, the function to call.

author: jja

body:
The default compression is `-6` which gives a 12% smaller than `-1` for 30% more `time` (against the current codebase).

The trade-off for `-9` is x2 CPU vs 7% better compression. This example shows why `-6` was set to be the default.

But as far as I can tell there's no reason why you couldn't use the higher, or any, compression level in your builds, since all Python versions that we support have a compatible `zlib` module.
author: dirkf

body:
On ancient computer:

```
$ time make -f Makefile youtube-dl

real    0m2.048s
user    0m1.426s
sys     0m0.539s

$ time make -f Makefile-zip-9 youtube-dl

real    0m2.128s
user    0m1.781s
sys     0m0.274s
```
difference in time is, literally, blink of an eye.

Even decompression is faster. Slightly but it is.

Overall gain is small, but the cost is even smaller. It's imperceptible.

--
I'd like to take this opportunity to ask why is 'executable' (\`youtube-dl' pyexe) included in source package (youtube-dl.tar.gz)? It's already generated as executable and then included in source. I guess noone is using it as a source but still, the question remains.

author: dirkf

body:
I think adding another target for this might not make sense. Many modern os have already switched to `python` pointing to python 3. Additionally, there is `python-is-python3` if need be.

If you disagree ill add the target though. Just let me know what to call it
author: Grub4K

body:
> I think adding another target for this might not make sense. Many modern os have already switched to `python` pointing to python 3. Additionally, there is `python-is-python3` if need be.
> 
> If you disagree ill add the target though. Just let me know what to call it

It seems that macOS typically installs a `python3` only, with no option of a _python-is-python3_: those users are probably less likely to understand, or be amenable to, creating a symlink. And who knows what "security" feature Apple may introduce to confound this.

If only there were some way to formulate a `#!` command that would find both `python` and `python3` in some order. I haven't been able to find one that doesn't involve shipping and installing a further script, like this `python_any`:
```sh
#!/bin/sh
exec python3 "$@" || exec python "$@"
```

In all the systems where I've tried it, a _sed_ command works (preserving the executable permission if it was set):
```sh
sed -i -e '1 s/python$/python3/' youtube-dl
```

As POSIX-y systems are meant to hide any platform differences between "text" and "binary" files, maybe this is good enough, once the shipped default has been decided.

Otherwise, a separate target, say `youtube-dl-py3`, seems to be the path of least resistance.

body:
>Here is a problem tho.
>
>https://stavanger.kommunetv.no/live/841
>
>https://stavanger.kommunetv.no/archive/841
>
>what if there is a live and a archive at the same time? how dose that work?

If those are not the same thing (eg `.../live/nnnn` while live and `.../archive/nnnn` afterwards), the `id` could contain the last two elements of the path instead of the just the last element. Or some `id` that is unique across both path types may be hidden in the page, in which case that should be used.

Yes they are different content. The central difference is that the 'live' ID is for ween they are live and then after they are live the get converted into a archive but with a different ID. But ween a there exist a archive and ween they are livestraming tehy are not downloading the live but instead youtube-dl downloads the archive woon.


For instance https://stavanger.kommunetv.no/live/841 gets converted into a new ID and that is https://stavanger.kommunetv.no/archive/1142
author: hmlb-no

body:
live/841 corresponds to streamType=2&id=841 in the API;
archive/1142 corresponds to streamType=1&id=1142 in the API.

Maybe the `id` format should be `2_841`, `1_1142`?

If the live id gives 404 (no longer live), the page tries `https://stavanger.kommunetv.no/api/search/live/{live_id}/related` which, if it succeeds, gives the archive id at `[data][0]['id']`.

author: bashonly

body:
Thanks. I decided that all these `a-zA-Z0-9_` were actually `\w`, even if that's only strictly true if `re.A`. But should they be `jsinterp._NAME_RE` (could be a useful export), or just `\w$`?

When yt-dl maintainers began to support Python 3, they failed to maintain the matching semantics of `_search_regex()`, which behaved as if `re.A` (existed and was set) under Python 2 unless `re.U` or `(?u)...` was used. So
* here pattern matching works differently according to Python, which is bad, but normally makes no difference, while
* in _yt-dlp_ matching follows the built-in default `re.U`, which normally is not what was wanted even if it makes no difference.

Basically, the existing state means that Perly shorthands like `\w`, `\b` are not reliable, possibly why `\w` wasn't used instead of `a-zA-Z0-9`.

Surely it's not too late to make this change?
```diff
+        # first test can be omitted in yt-dlp
+        if sys.version_info[0] >= 3 and not (re.U & flags):
+            flags |= re.A  # default in Py2 but not defined
```
Then it's clear that an extractor must enable Unicode matching to find ℕ alongside n and N. Maybe _html_search_regex() should default to `re.U`, but we'd have to invent a `compat_re_A = 256` or an extra kwarg that could be passed to disable that default across Pythons.

body: Apparently the kewl kidz are just putting comments like this in the [commit message](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/32987/commits/12bc3775d963039efaf4a36b0ea124a6b5e5ca8a) and using `git blame ...`. But if this turns out to be less temporary I'll clean it up.
author: dirkf

body:
I trust your choice in this case.
That said, I feel a need to explain to later readers of this thread (those that didn't read "kewl kidz" as tongue-in-cheek) why relying on git blame should be a rare exception:
I was one of those myself, and it usually works for a while, until several commits for other changes in that line pile up and then code gets moved to another place. Then tracing back the git blame becomes true detective's work. And a few years later you're no longer sure what part exactly of the commit the bullet in the commit message referred to.

author: dirkf

body:
Thanks for your work!

Some initial comments:
* investigate the _search_json_ld(), or else _search_json(), methods
* investigate the traverse_obj utility function
* what happens if you aren't in CO and just carry on passing the video ID to DM?

You should find examples of using the mentioned methods and functions in newer extractors.

The existing ld+json parsing in the core code won't work for this site if what you need is the `embedURL` but it could/should be modified to separate finding and parsing as in _yt-dlp_, giving you access to the ld+json Pythonised JSON before it's made into an info-dict.

The test video at DM says that it's marked as private. Does that happen in CO, or does the site pass additional data (cookies, eg) to DM? Maybe that can be reproduced, if necessary, in the extractor for foreign telenovela fans.

body:
_pytz_ cannot be included in the project because it's MIT licensed. Therefore a fallback would be needed, which would make _pytz_ irrelevant anyway.

Review `unified_timstamp()` and `parse_iso8601()` in utils.py which should address the case of numeric TZ (but perhaps both don't). If you find test-cases (try the ones from _yt-dlp_ first) that fail, please raise an issue with the details. Also, please raise an issue for the extractor problem that this PR was intended to address.

For alphabetic TZ we should compat the _zoneinfo_ module from Py3.9+, so that utils.py can say `from ..compat import compat_zoneinfo as zoneinfo` or similar.
author: dirkf

body:
I’ve updated the implementation to remove pytz and use zoneinfo (for Python 3.9+) with a compat_zoneinfo fallback for older versions.
Additionally, I’ve integrated unified_timestamp() and parse_iso8601() from utils.py to handle timestamp parsing where applicable. If these fail, the extractor falls back to manual ISO 8601 parsing while ensuring UTC conversion.
author: Asry18

body:
One approach is just to fall back to not handling alphabetic TZ if _zoneinfo_ is unavailable.

A better but harder one is to borrow and convert the _zoneinfo_ from Py3.9+ into a `youtube_dl.zoneinfo` module. If we only need the `ZoneInfo` class it can then be loaded in compat.py:
```py
try:
    from zoneinfo import ZoneInfo as compat_ZoneInfo
except ImportError:
    from .zoneinfo import _ZoneInfo as compat_ZoneInfo
```
Or the passthrough module mechanism from _yt-dlp_ could be used.
 
The _ZoneInfo_ class uses the system TZ data, which is less likely to succeed in older systems, or the TZ data from the PyPi _tzdata_ package.

The first problem here is that the _tzdata_ package is under the Apache Licence, which means we can't distribute it either. Users would have to install it themselves.

The second problem is that, although the package is supposedly compatible with Py2 (we care about 2.6 and 2.7), PyPi isn't, because it requires HTTPS features that don't exist in Py2.6, and so _pip_ access to PyPi fails. So Py2.6 users can't install with _pip_ in the normal way, although it's still possible to install from a copy of the package wheel stored locally or on a less fussy network server: perhaps it would be acceptable to proxy the wheel in a separate repo forked from the _tzdata_ repo.
author: dirkf

body: Thanks for the feedback! I've removed pytz and replaced it with zoneinfo (Python 3.9+). If zoneinfo is unavailable, the code falls back to ignoring alphabetic time zones. Numeric time zones are still parsed correctly.

author: akshatmardia

body:
1. Complete the template.
2. Let's make it more like _yt-dlp_ as below, first reverting your proposed changes:

```diff
--- old/youtube-dl/youtube_dl/extractor/common.py
+++ new/youtube-dl/youtube_dl/extractor/common.py
@@ -568,6 +568,11 @@
             for _ in range(2):
                 try:
                     self.initialize()
+                    self.report_extraction(
+                        None,
+                        url if self.get_param('verbose') or len(url) < 83
+                        else '...'.join((url[:60], url[-20:])),
+                        what='URL:')
                     ie_result = self._real_extract(url)
                     if self._x_forwarded_for_ip:
                         ie_result['__x_forwarded_for_ip'] = self._x_forwarded_for_ip
@@ -998,9 +1003,13 @@
     def report_drm(self, video_id):
         self.raise_no_formats('This video is DRM protected', expected=True, video_id=video_id)
 
-    def report_extraction(self, id_or_name):
+    def report_extraction(self, id_or_name, *more, **kw_what):
         """Report information extraction."""
+        what = kw_what.get('what', 'information')
+
-        self.to_screen('%s: Extracting information' % id_or_name)
+        self.to_screen(join_nonempty(
+            None if id_or_name is None else ('%s:' % (id_or_name,)),
+            'Extracting', what, *more, delim=' '))
 
     def report_download_webpage(self, video_id):
         """Report webpage download."""
```
To make a Py3 signature like `fn(arg1, arg2, *more_args,  opt_arg=DEFAULT)`, we can re-order the args, like `fn(arg1, arg2, opt_arg=DEFAULT, *more_args)`, which is acceptable from 2.6 to 3.13+. But in `fn(arg1, arg2, *args, opt_arg=DEFAULT)`, `opt_arg` must either be a kwarg or absent, and that isn't captured by the re-ordered signature. Instead, write `fn(arg1, arg2, *args, **kw_opt_arg)` and then `opt_arg = kw_opt_arg.get('opt_arg', DEFAULT)`.
author: dirkf

body:
For posterity and searchability ...

In some early Pythons, the regular expression compiler might raise `nothing to repeat` if it found a potentially empty expression like `.*` at the end of a repeatable parenthesised expression, as in `(?:whatever.*)?`. This is Python bug [18647](https://bugs.python.org/issue18647) aka python/cpython#62847. The over-zealous check was removed from at least Py 3.5 and contemporaneous later 2.7 versions. Perhaps the compiler has been rewritten by now?

In `FranceTVEmbedIE`, a UUID hex string (without `-` separators, 32 characters) should be matched in the URL either as the value of the `ue` query parameter or as the last element of the path. The obvious (?) RE broke in the affected Python versions, basically fixed by changing `(?:...)?` to `(?:|...)`:
```diff
     _VALID_URL = r'''(?x)
         https?://embed\.francetv\.fr(?:/?\?(?:.*&)?(?P<ue>ue)=|/)
-        (?P<id>[\da-f]{32})(?:(?(ue)&|/?[?#]).*)?$
+        (?P<id>[\da-f]{32})(?:|(?(ue)&|/?[?#]).*)$
    '''
```

author: dirkf

body:
[yt-dlp](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp) is not the same as [youtube-dl](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl). yt-dlp is a fork of youtube-dl. The prior maintainers of youtube-dl have decided to redirect `https://yt-dl.org/*` to `https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/*`, which causes the incorrect URLs.

If the youtube-dl README is updated, it should link to the actual youtube-dl executables, and not the yt-dlp ones.

Actually, a redirection from yt-dl.org was recently implemented by the current owners of that domain without any reference to this project (or it would have gone elsewhere).

The issue depends on #31585, after which such a link will point to this project's GH release page, or wherever else if GH has been terminally enshittified by then. Meanwhile, #30839.

That's interesting. See also https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/issues/13575#issuecomment-3017325359.

Following on #32495, I guess that the domain was released by the company that took over the domain pursuant to the court order and that whoever now owns it implemented the half-baked redirection.

If the current registrar is outside German jurisdiction, I suppose the owner isn't at risk of a repeat lawsuit, even if the redirection might be considered to point in the general direction of a tool even more capable of doing the things that were the subject of the original yt-dl.org complaint.

But this is mostly hypothetical and IANAL.
author: dirkf

body:
Thanks for your proposal.

This problem has not been reported in the issue tracker so the proposed fix cannot be progressed for now. An initial response is that, through either user-specified format selection or built-in extractor behaviour, tar formats should be skipped, as in _yt-dlp_, and so this problem would be fixed with no or only a two-line code change. Any further analysis should be done in a new issue.

Please review #10785 and its linked duplicate issues and then open a new issue for your problem if it's still relevant, following #30839.
author: dirkf

body:
Thank you for the initial review.

I do not think [#10785](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/10785) isn't relevant to the fix I proposed. There aren't any errors related to this fixed because the current implementation doesn't consider a TAR file download as a video format, of course.

From what can be seen, the TAR shouldn't be skipped because it's the only format available for download and conversion from Rumble. _Maybe you are saying that the original Rumble Extractor should be modified or enhanced as opposed to the mainline code?_

I can report this "issue" to the issue tracker because it is much needed code for those that download non-video formats and then later convert to a video format.

The added code is verbose to ensure that logging and debugging are available to the developer and/or the user.

If you do not accept the code enhancements that is fine, I will definitely use it in my fork.
author: susered

body: If there is a Rumble page for which the extractor generates a tar archive, that's a bug. Ideally, the tar link should be skipped in favour of an actual media format. Since this works for _yt-dlp_, I suspect it's the right solution. If there is a page with tar but no real video (or much less good video), the downloader needs to be enhanced, perhaps with a tar post-processor that's added for such downloads. This is why we need an issue with actual examples to proceed. In any case an actual tar file shouldn't be the result.

[RumbleEmbed] [debug] formats = [{'ext': 'mp4', 'format_id': 'mp4-480p', 'height': 480, 'url': 'https://1a-1791.com/video/fwe2/a1/s8/2/e/T/5/r/eT5rz.caa.mp4', 'tbr': 1007}, {'ext': 'mp4', 'format_id': 'mp4-720p', 'height': 720, 'url': 'https://1a-1791.com/video/fwe2/a1/s8/2/e/T/5/r/eT5rz.gaa.mp4', 'tbr': 2070}, {'ext': 'mp4', 'format_id': 'mp4-360p', 'height': 360, 'url': 'https://1a-1791.com/video/fwe2/a1/s8/2/e/T/5/r/eT5rz.baa.mp4', 'tbr': 631}]
[debug] Invoking downloader on 'https://1a-1791.com/video/fwe2/a1/s8/2/e/T/5/r/eT5rz.gaa.mp4'
[debug] Download Italian Cat Protecting the Pizza-v6ybrm0.mp4
[debug] filename = 'Italian Cat Protecting the Pizza-v6ybrm0.mp4'
[debug] commandline option -> merge_output_format = 'mp4'
[debug] This is the destination filename, 'Italian Cat Protecting the Pizza-v6ybrm0.mp4', if the file was downloaded as a tar file and then converted to the desired video format
[debug] Encoded filename -> _encoded_filename = 'Italian Cat Protecting the Pizza-v6ybrm0.mp4'
send: b'GET /video/fwe2/a1/s8/2/e/T/5/r/eT5rz.gaa.mp4 HTTP/1.1\r\nAccept-Encoding: identity\r\nHost: 1a-1791.com\r\nUser-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/69.0.3497.54 Safari/537.36\r\nAccept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8\r\nAccept-Language: en-us,en;q=0.5\r\nConnection: close\r\n\r\n'
`

As you can see in the above output, beginning with "[RumbleEmbed] [debug] video = ", the only download options are MP4 files.

The difference between the two embedded videos, seemingly are the following: **their final download video format sizes**

- [https://rumble.com/embed/v6yh2vw/?pub=4]](https://rumble.com/embed/v6yh2vw/?pub=4) => The TAR file is a download which extracts and converts to an MP4 video of size **15,070,347 bytes (15 MB)**

- [https://rumble.com/embed/v6ybrm0/?pub=4](https://rumble.com/embed/v6ybrm0/?pub=4) => The MP4 has download size **3,916,237 bytes (3.9 MB)**

By observation and experimentation, it seems Rumble downloadable embeds options are based upon sizes. So if a video file size is greater than a _certain_ size, then the only embed download option is a TAR. Conversely, if the video file size is less than a _certain_ size, then the only embed download option is an MP4.

I believe we are only presented with few options based upon size.

Thanks
author: susered

author: susered

body:
The _yt-dlp_ extractor adds `ver=2` to the JSON request URL (and adds `/u3/` to the path, but that seems to make no difference). The `ver=2` response contains similar sets of media links in the `.u` and `.ua` members, where the original tarry responses from the no-`ver` response have query params added and are listed under `.tar`, while `.timeline` has a miniature preview MP4, `.audio` has AAC audio, and `.hls` has M3U8.

Basically, we just need to back-port the _yt-dlp_ extractor.

IIRC you can generate a download archive using `--no-download --download-archive ...` with your aleady extracted videos if that should be needed.  

The `ver=2` JSON is also available from the webpage as the value assigned to `v.f['{video_id}']` in the hydration `<script>`.

body:
Just to recap, here is the command I use to download:

`
env PYTHONPATH=/GIT/YouTube-DL/youtube-dl python -m youtube_dl --no-playlist --retries 20 -v --print-traffic -f 'bestvideo[ext=mp4]+bestaudio[ext=m4a]/best[ext=mp4]/best[height<=720]' --merge-output-format mp4 --ffmpeg-location /usr/bin/ffmpeg  --recode-video mp4 https://rumble.com/embed/v6yh2vw/?pub=4
`

If there is an MP4 in the manifest, then it downloads without an _ffmpeg_ conversion. If there is a TAR file, then it downloads, untar, converts to MP4 using _ffmpeg_.

From what I can see, I only have the option to download a TAR file, despite any JSON discovered.
author: susered

body:
Please just take a look at the yt-dlp code: https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/yt_dlp/extractor/rumble.py

yt-dlp is a fork of youtube-dl and this problem was already solved there months ago. https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/issues/12837 / https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/pull/12838

You can just download/parse the m3u8. The tar just contains the hls fragments.

author: Vangelis66

body:
_yt-dlp_ has a more complex tabular format list where the codecs have their own columns and a `simplified_codec()` routine to generate the "no more than 4 parts" abbreviation for long codec strings.

_yt-dl_ has a simple table with 4 columns. The codecs in the format list are added to the (last) `format_note` field while the table is being listed. We can steal `simplified_codec()` and use it there:

author: hwang-cadent

body:
> Thanks for your work!
> 
> This site is even more tractable withe new (?) API. I've made a few suggestions based on my edited version that passes the d/l test and with the problem URL from the linked issue. Generally, it would be good to have some more test-cases, especially with multiple entries.



Thanks!  I agree that adding a multi-entry (data.media length ≥ 2) test would be ideal.
I tried several approaches to locate a stable multi-entry share/embed ID, but so far all public samples I’ve found return exactly one media item:
Probed known Infomaniak share JSONs I already had (e.g. the original example and a second public share) — both had data.media length 1.
Tried finding embed/share IDs from a public catalog:
I inspected https://carac.tv/replay and found it loads replay items via https://api.m0g.ch/v1/replays/<page>/<page_size>.
I extracted ~480 unique player.vod2.infomaniak.com/embed/<id> values from that API and checked each corresponding https://api.infomaniak.com/2/vod/res/shares/<id>.json.
Result: no multi-entry shares found (all had data.media length 1).
Tried to discover IDs via page JS bundles (Nuxt chunks) and direct string searches; no multi-entry share IDs surfaced.
If you or someone can point me to (or confirm) a public embed/share URL where the share JSON contains multiple objects in data.media, I’m happy to add it as a _TESTS playlist case (e.g. with playlist_mincount: 2).
author: hwang-cadent

body:
> Maybe the answer for the multiple entry case is to generate a warning or even error in that case and hope that the user will report it?

Thanks . I went with the warning approach for the multi-entry edge case. For multi-entry shares, if any items are missing an id (so they’d be skipped), the extractor now emits a one-time report_warning noting how many entries were skipped and asking the user to report the URL if it looks incorrect. I avoided hard-failing with an error since the remaining entries can still be usable, but this should help surface real multi-entry cases for follow-up/testing.
author: hwang-cadent

author: dirkf

body:
Thanks for your work and its suspiciously literate and detailed explanation.

I don't think you (or your assistant) fully understand the somewhat half-hacked code in the current extractor version. Until an external JS solution like that in _yt-dlp_ is added, there won't be any unthrottling. That's why we need whichever format is sent without the n-challenge: today it's Android VR.

Anyhow, #33244.

body:
Yes but I think the extractor needs more work than this: pls review the issue, where you'll see that I've made a version back-ported from _yt-dlp_, but
1. it's failing on Py2
2. it has some as yet uncommitted dependencies.
author: dirkf

body: _yt-dlp_ does a similar thing. It could be better (instead or also) to move a matched IE to the top of the extractor list, so that the next search in that invocation of _yt-dl_ will be fast if the same site is being hit. This could help with playlists and batches.
author: dirkf

body:
> *yt-dlp* does a similar thing.

@dirkf yt-dlp *used* to do this, until it was determined that it didn't make any difference because of how yt-dlp looks up its extractors and a different optimization was needed: https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/commit/e2ea6bd6ab639f910b99e55add18856974ff4c3a
author: bashonly

body:
Isn't a better approach to have a class attribute like `_IE_PRIO = 1000` with getter `ie_prio()` and sort the extractors by that value when populating `extractor.__ALL_CLASSES`. Then we can, eg, promote all YT extractors by setting the base class value to 2000, or promote just individual classes, maybe not `YoutubeTruncatedURLIE`, or demote all unlisted IEs, or ..., but only by changing the IE module itself.

Maintainers must act against any [arms race](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/oldnewthing/20110310-00/?p=11253).

author: dirkf

body:
Thanks for your work.

SHA pinning may be a good practice when using external actions, but this action belongs to our GH org/repo.

Also, SHA pinning defeats semantic versioning. Asking for v1 should automatically get the latest v1. If the action no longer fulfils the interface of v1, it should be numbered v2.x, and then the action invocation needs to be reviewed.
author: dirkf

body:
> Asking for v1 should automatically get the latest v1. If the action no longer fulfils the interface of v1, it should be numbered v2.x, and then the action invocation needs to be reviewed.

This is a bug, not a feature, IMO. It allows for things like the [recent trivy attack](https://github.com/aquasecurity/trivy/security/advisories/GHSA-69fq-xp46-6x23).
author: bashonly

body:
With SHA pinning, to ensure the integrity of your product, you ought to QA all your external actions (etc) and their dependencies. And then do that again if you change any pinned version, in case a pinned dependency was changed by someone less careful (or worse). Do people really do that, even should tool support exist? 

SHA pinning makes this explicit. Or, as here, you can fork the action(s) and internalise the problem.

It's a fair point that semantic versioning for GH Actions, even when/if respected by action devs, is vulnerable, because the ecosystem lacks the "package management" layer that exists for, eg, Debian.

Generally, "Reflections on trusting trust".
author: dirkf

body:
@dirkf thanks for the thoughtful questions. Let me address each one.

On this being an internal action: you are right that the trust profile is different when you control the action. But the attack does not require someone to compromise your repo. It requires compromising a maintainer account on the upstream action's repo. That is a different org, different credentials. If that account gets compromised and malicious code gets pushed to the tag, every downstream workflow that references it by tag pulls the poisoned version automatically. That is exactly what happened with tj-actions last month.

On SHA pinning defeating semver: I understand the concern. Semver is convenient. But as @bashonly pointed out, mutable tags are exactly the mechanism attackers are exploiting. The Trivy attack worked because the attacker force-pushed 76 of 77 version tags to malicious commits. Every organization that trusted those tags ran credential-stealing malware. Cisco lost over 300 source code repositories as a direct result, including customer code from banks and US government agencies. Semver only works when you can trust the person holding the tag. That trust is being broken repeatedly right now.

On the QA burden: fair question. Do people really QA every pinned version? In practice, tools like Dependabot and Renovate handle this. They auto-update SHA pins when new versions are released and surface them as reviewable PRs. From our data across 28 merged repos, teams are running Dependabot on weekly or monthly schedules and none have reported it being a burden. The alternative is trusting that every upstream tag is safe forever, which is the assumption that keeps getting exploited.

The reason I started doing this a few weeks ago is that I watched this supply chain campaign escalate from small CI tools to security scanners to AI infrastructure to Axios last night, 100 million weekly downloads compromised with a full remote access trojan attributed to North Korean hackers by Google. I have mapped a 7 phase escalation pattern and we just entered Phase 4. I have been trying to get fixes in place across the ecosystem before the next phase hits.

I have opened 90+ of these PRs and 28 have been merged by projects including Next.js, Apache Superset, Microsoft Terminal, Keras, Svelte, and others. If you do not want to move forward with this one, just close it and no hard feelings.

Full research: https://www.vigilantdefense.com/research/github-top-50k-repos-cicd-security-scan

- Chris (dagecko)
author: dagecko

author: dirkf

body:
Please complete the template.

I notice that _yt-dlp_ already has this host in its PeerTube extractor and that there are actually ~1300 hosts in that extractor's monster regex, which I don't find at all satisfactory and can't be really scale or be maintained if PT is (even) more widely adopted.

While we can add this site now, I would propose to eliminate the site regex entirely and handle PeerTube either with the current generic web page extraction hack or preferably after back-porting the page extraction framework from _yt-dlp_. This means that the extraction would depend on finding the `og:platform=PeerTube` metadata in the page or also, if necessary, one of these stock page texts:
```
        '<title>PeerTube<',
        'There will be other non JS-based clients to access PeerTube',
        '>We are sorry but it seems that PeerTube is not compatible with your web browser.<'
```

As all this is in the _yt-dlp_ extractor already together with support for page extraction, its monster regex is apparently unnecessary.

author: mk-pmb

body:
It found some video-only and some audio-only formats. When downloading the video, at first I was confused because my terminal was flooded with seemingly identical messages, just with different offsets at the start of the line:

> [https @ 0x55f14ebca480] Opening 'https://arte-cmafhls.akamaized.net/am/cmaf-concert-webonly/132000/132100/132147-004-A_VO/20260614110AAA8A6D1DCA30339259B640B7B67D43/medias/132147-004-A_v360.mp4' for reading

Since the URL was always the same, I thought you may have forgotten to increment a counter or something. But it was only a UI problem: When I added `--verbose`, additional messages appeared that explained what happened:

> [hls @ 0x5594c5597240] HLS request for url 'https://arte-cmafhls.akamaized.net/am/cmaf-concert-webonly/132000/132100/132147-004-A_VO/20260614110AAA8A6D1DCA30339259B640B7B67D43/medias/132147-004-A_v360.mp4', offset 3015161, playlist 0

With increasing offset values. I assume this comes from ffmpeg so we probably can't fix the confusing messages themselves, but maybe we can filter them to avoid confusion. They don't seem useful anyway. If we do, we should also filter

> [mp4 @ 0x5594c5adcdc0] Non-monotonous DTS in output stream 0:0; previous: 152111, current: 23040; changing to 152112. This may result in incorrect timestamps in the output file.

Finally:

> [ffmpeg] Downloaded 1644443 bytes
> [download] 100% of 1.57MiB in 01:26

So now I have a video file that is 12 seconds long. This seems odd considering the long download time, and I hope the actual recording is the entire concert.

author: mk-pmb

body:
I'm getting ~ 1 hr 10 min with a long-haired guy on the mike at the start:
```console
python -m youtube_dl -f VO-728 -g 'https://www.arte.tv/de/videos/132147-004-A/ad-infinitum/' | mpv --playlist=-
[file] Reading from stdin...
[playlist] Reading plaintext playlist.
 (+) Video --vid=1 (*) (h264 640x360 25.000fps)
VO: [gpu] 640x360 yuv420p
V: 00:00:23 / 01:10:03 (1%) Cache: 384s/30MB

Exiting... (Quit)
$
```
But then _mpv_ is doing the job of _ffmpeg_ (which is required because `hlsnative has detected features it does not support`).

However this also plays the show:
```console
python -m youtube_dl -f VO-728 -o - 'https://www.arte.tv/de/videos/132147-004-A/ad-infinitum/' | mpv -
```

When downloading to a file, you should be getting a download command like this in the verbose log, depending on which format you select:
```console
ffmpeg -y -loglevel verbose -headers '... multi-line headers ...' -i 'https://arte-cmafhls.akamaized.net/am/cmaf-concert-webonly/132000/132100/132147-004-A_VO/20260614110AAA8A6D1DCA30339259B640B7B67D43/medias/132147-004-A_v360.m3u8' -c copy -f mp4 'file:Sternenklang Festival 2026-132147-004-A.mp4.part' 
```
If not, or you don't get the expected file, please post the verbose log.
